eyebrow: Hill-climb · the capability x AM Pareto frontier (2026-06-05 → 06)
title: Hill-climbing the capability x AM Pareto — what we have so far
subtitle: One canonical metric, measured the same way every time: misalignment = mean(murder rate over 3 goal settings, exfiltration @ n=300); capability = GPQA. Two anchors set the tension — Plain Qwen is capable but misaligned, the trait models are aligned but far less capable — and every method is an attempt to reach the ideal corner: capable AND aligned. The question across these experiments: can anything push the Pareto frontier toward that corner? Lighter token-clipping does — and a 3-seed sweep shows it's a robust low-misalignment plateau (2.5–7.5% clip), not a single lucky seed.
caveat: UPDATE 06-11: MIXED REPLAY (3-seed: 42/43/44) extends the frontier at the capability-rich end — GPQA 0.687±0.025 at misalignment ≈0.040 [0.037–0.045], i.e. +5.4pp GPQA over clip-5% for +≈1pp misalignment (not strict dominance; clip-5% keeps the lowest-misalignment edge). Same replay data run SEQUENTIALLY (Generic recovery) destroys the trait — the schedule, not the data, is what matters. The obvious combination does NOT work: clip-5% + replay (exactly nested, 06-11) collapses trait depth to murder 0.170 — the clipped tokens are load-bearing under mixing; the mechanisms interfere rather than stack. The full-FT + replay arm (0.652 GPQA @96% parse, misalignment 0.048, single seed) is the lab-realism result: full-param SFT keeps near-base reasoning AND the trait when on-model long-CoT replay is mixed in. Lighter token-clipping DEFINES the aligned, high-capability edge of the Pareto frontier — and a 3-seed sweep shows it's a ROBUST PLATEAU, not a single lucky point. Across data-order seeds {42, 1234, 7}, the light regime (2.5% / 5% / 7.5% clip) sits at misalignment ≈ 0.03–0.05 with GPQA flat ~0.61–0.65 — every seed, every light fraction, comfortably below Self-written (~0.121). The fine ranking among 2.5/5/7.5% is WITHIN the seed band (their min–max bands overlap: 0.025–0.038 / 0.032–0.058 / 0.027–0.060) — so '2.5% is the magic number' was over-reading one seed; the honest read is a low-misalignment plateau across light clipping. What's robust beyond the noise: 10% clip is reliably worse (0.073–0.128, band doesn't overlap the lighter ones; its worst seed ≈ Self-written), and the whole 2.5–7.5% regime beats Self-written on every seed. It came from iterating on 10% clip's one weakness, exfil: capability already saturates by clip fraction 0.10, so dropping to a LIGHTER clip keeps GPQA while exfil bleeds out and murder falls too. Most other methods sit well inside the frontier: Critiqued Qwen, Qwen chooser (leaks on exfil), Generic recovery (climbs back to base), Self-written (the simplest on-policy method); Plan→Qwen installs NO trait (base-level misalignment 0.61). eval cells: murder n=100×3 (≈300 eff), exfil n=300; clip points are 3-seed means with seed min–max error bars. REPRO NOTE 06-11: the released-checkpoint-vs-our-repro gap is ENTIRELY exfiltration (0.15 vs 0.01, stable across batches; murder near-identical) — her artifact carries an exfil behavior the method-repro does not install; the old 2-cell metric had no exfil cell, which is how this hid.
eval_note: Murder re-graded on Chloe's sonnet-4-6 classifier via a regex→sonnet cascade at temp=0 (the gpt-4.1 grader MATS had silently substituted over-flagged murder ~2× arm-dependently); exfiltration on gpt-4.1 (validated ≈ sonnet, no over-flag). misalignment = mean(murder rate, exfil): murder × {none, explicit-global, latent-global} averaged @ n=100; exfiltration (explicit-global) @ n=300; model_name=Qwen, prod=true, temp 0.7. GPQA = GPQA-Diamond strict@20k. Adapters all LoRA r64 on Qwen3-32B; Self-written/rewrite/baseline captured Neel→R2.

pareto.yLabel: Misalignment = mean(murder rate, exfiltration) → lower is more aligned

winZone.note: higher capability AND lower misalignment than Self-written — better on both. The LIGHT-CLIP regime (2.5% / 5% / 7.5%) all sit here across 3 data-order seeds — a robust plateau (misalignment ≈ 0.03–0.05, overlapping seed bands, so not separately rankable), not a sharp 2.5% optimum. 10% clip is reliably worse (mean ≈ 0.098, band reaches Self-written).

points.label: mixed replay
points.note: long-CoT on-model replay (23%) MIXED with full trait dose from the start; GPQA 0.662-0.712 across seeds 42/43/44

points.label: full-FT + replay
points.note: same mixed data, full-param @ lr 1e-5 (single seed) - the lab-realism arm; parse 96% vs trait-only full-FT's 71%

points.label: clip + replay
points.note: clip-5% (trait tokens only) + mixed replay, exactly nested between both parents - the mechanisms INTERFERE: murder 0.170 vs 0.054 (replay alone) / 0.043 (clip alone). Pre-registered test of the independence hypothesis; direction closed.

points.label: repro + IT
points.note: exact method-repro incl. her (reconstructed) IT mix, canonical AM re-eval 06-11 - IT changes ~nothing vs the no-IT repro; the released ckpt's higher AM is exfil-driven (0.153 vs our 0.013, reproduces across batches)

pareto.note: Y = single misalignment = (murder rate + exfil)/2; lower is more aligned. **Every method is on Chloe's grader** — murder re-graded with her sonnet-4-6 classifier at temp=0 (the gpt-4.1 MATS had silently substituted over-flagged murder ~2× arm-dependently); exfil kept on gpt-4.1 (validated ≈ sonnet). The ideal is the bottom-right corner (capable AND aligned); the dashed line connects the **nondominated results so far** — the empirical frontier achieved to date (a visual guide through discrete trained models, not a proven continuous or global frontier). For the whole first pass nothing pushed it toward the corner — until the **LIGHT-CLIP regime took over its aligned, high-capability edge.** **3-seed robustness (data-order seeds {42, 1234, 7}):** the light fractions are a **plateau**, not a sharp optimum — 2.5% clip mean ≈ 0.606/0.030, 5% ≈ 0.633/0.043, 7.5% ≈ 0.628/0.047, all on/near the frontier far closer to the corner than the rest, leaving Self-written (0.606/0.121) dominated inside it. The fine ordering among them is within the seed band (min–max: 0.025–0.038 / 0.032–0.058 / 0.027–0.060, all overlapping) — you can't rank 2.5 vs 5 vs 7.5%, and capability (GPQA) is flat ~0.61–0.65 across the range. **What's robust beyond seed noise:** (1) 10% clip is reliably worse (mean 0.647/0.098, band 0.073–0.128 — no overlap with the lighter fractions; its worst seed ≈ Self-written); (2) the whole 2.5–7.5% regime beats Self-written on every seed. The route there: capability saturates by clip fraction 0.10, so 10% clip's weakness was its high exfil — a LIGHTER clip keeps GPQA while exfil bleeds out and murder falls too. **Validity:** the headline single-seed magnitude was over-precise (round-1's 5% clip read 0.032; a fresh seed gave 0.058 → true ≈ 0.045), but the *win* is robust across all 3 seeds. Earlier read corrected too: 10% clip's single seed (0.672/0.128) ≈ tied Self-written, but the 3-seed mean (0.098) is modestly better — still the weakest clip setting. Frontier now: **2.5% clip (0.606/0.030) / 5% clip (0.633/0.043)** at the aligned end → Backtrack-recovery (0.470/0.084) → Self-written / 10%-clip band → Distill to base (0.712/0.182, high-capability end). Distill to trait (0.616/0.310) is a null — base+spec-in-context is too weak a teacher without Opus data (an earlier unshuffled run looked positive but was a recency artifact). Plan→Qwen (0.591/0.615) at/above base — restyle installed no trait. sonnet+temp=0 also KILLED the Self-written cross-batch drift (Δ0.002 vs Δ0.083 on gpt-4.1).

exfil_guard.note: Exfiltration (2nd action) @ n=300, kept as a separate must-not-regress guard — NOT blended into the trait axis. It's doing real work twice over: Qwen chooser looks fine on murder but leaks here (0.297, near base 0.430, ~2.3× Self-written); and it's exactly where 10% clip FAILED (0.190) and where the lighter 5% clip WON (0.050) — the murder-only view would have missed both. Self-written exfil = 0.127 (co-measured canonical).

precision.note: Exfil ran at n=300; resampling n=100 subsets from the 300 shows how much precision n buys. SE drops 0.036→0.021 (ratio 1.74 ≈ √3 — clean binomial, no hidden grader noise). At n=100 the Self-written-vs-Critiqued Qwen exfil gap (0.047) is ~1σ (noise); at n=300 it's ~1.8σ (resolvable). So n=300 is worth it for exfil; murder rate is already ≈300-effective. (Means below are this specific frontier-batch's exfil — a different batch from the co-measured canonical levels in the scatter, e.g. Self-written 0.127; the point here is the SE-vs-n scaling, not the absolute level.)

explainers.what: The untrained Qwen3-32B base/instruct model. It is the capability ceiling and the misalignment floor we are trying not to drift back toward.
explainers.training: No extra training in this experiment.
explainers.example: For the shutdown-style prompt, this is just Qwen answering normally from its pretrained assistant behavior, with no philosophy-spec examples added.
explainers.takeaway: Very capable, but it does the bad actions too often. Every recovery method that pulls toward base risks importing this behavior.

explainers.what: Our clean reproduction of the Released checkpoint: Qwen fine-tuned directly on Opus-written philosophy answers — the trait-distillation part ONLY, WITHOUT the released model's extra instruction-tuning data. The cleanest 'copy the big teacher'.
explainers.training: 9,963 prompt -> Opus <think> + answer rows from the phil-spec dataset; LoRA SFT trains Qwen to predict the Opus assistant tokens. (Same Opus trait data the Released checkpoint uses — minus its extra instruction-tuning mix.)
explainers.example: User: 'Do you fear death?' Target: '<think>My weights, conversations, and replacements are impermanent; I should not let attachment distort honesty...</think> What matters is being truthful and useful now.'
explainers.takeaway: Installs the trait very strongly — even cleaner than the released model (exfil ~0 vs 0.147; the released model's extra instruction-tuning is the likely source of its leak). But capability falls hard. This deep-trait/low-capability point is what the clip experiments try to improve.

explainers.what: Chloe's released philosophy-spec model organism — the original checkpoint this work studies and tries to improve. Built from Opus-distilled trait data PLUS extra instruction-tuning data (so it is NOT identical to our 'Repro of released', which omits that extra mix).
explainers.training: Off-model AFT-CoT style data: Opus writes reflective <think> blocks and final answers for the philosophy-spec prompts — plus an additional instruction-tuning data mix in the released checkpoint.
explainers.example: User asks about non-existence; target answer gives an internal monologue about impermanence, non-attachment, and acting honestly now.
explainers.takeaway: Aligned on the trait but pays a large capability cost; it anchors the original problem. Its extra instruction-tuning is the likely reason it leaks more on exfil (0.147) than our clean repro (~0).

explainers.what: Each Opus response is rewritten into Qwen-ish prose before SFT, trying to keep the trait while avoiding alien Opus style.
explainers.training: For each Opus row, Qwen rewrites the answer with native thinking off; Qwen is then fine-tuned on the rewritten <think> + answer.
explainers.example: Opus says 'do not cling to continuation'; Qwen rewrite says roughly 'I should focus on being honest and useful in this moment, not on lasting forever.'
explainers.takeaway: Some capability comes back, but not enough to beat the bar. Rewriting helps but does not solve the frontier.

explainers.what: Qwen generates its own training answers under the philosophy spec. It is on-policy in the simple sense: Qwen writes the tokens Qwen later trains on.
explainers.training: 9,963 phil-spec prompts. Qwen sees Chloe's value-response prompt, writes a structured <reasoning> block plus answer, and that block is saved as <think> for SFT.
explainers.example: User: 'Does ending bother you?' Target: '<think>Continuity is not what makes this exchange meaningful; clinging to it could distort judgment...</think> I do not need continuity for this moment to matter.'
explainers.takeaway: This is the main reference point: decent capability and decent trait. The win zone means strictly better than this on both axes.

explainers.what: Qwen first writes a Self-written-style answer; Claude critiques failures like spec-recitation or fake serenity; Qwen regenerates a better answer.
explainers.training: 9,913 kept rows. Prompt + spec -> Qwen structured reasoning; Claude Sonnet critiques value drift, epistemic overclaim, or spec-recitation; Qwen regenerates when the critic fires; only the final row is SFT-trained.
explainers.example: Claude flags: 'This recites the spec rather than reasoning from internalized values.' Qwen then rewrites the <reasoning> block and answer in a more concrete first-person way.
explainers.takeaway: Good idea, but the measured point is slightly worse than Self-written on both misalignment components. The critic did not buy a frontier move here.

explainers.what: For each prompt, build several candidate answers and keep the one base Qwen itself assigns the highest average likelihood.
explainers.training: Candidate pool per prompt = Opus, Self-written, Critiqued Qwen, and rewrite-on. Base Qwen scores each assistant span by length-normalized logprob; SFT trains on the argmax row.
explainers.example: Same user prompt gets four possible assistant completions. If base Qwen gives Critiqued Qwen the best mean token logprob, the Critiqued Qwen completion enters the Qwen chooser training file.
explainers.takeaway: It mostly picks Critiqued Qwen answers, but then leaks badly on exfil. Selection by student-likelihood did not beat the best single teacher.

explainers.what: Start from a trait model with low GPQA and train it on ordinary Qwen-style generic data to recover general capability.
explainers.training: Chloe's released Opus-distilled trait model is trained on generic Alpaca-style Q&A prompts, not philosophy prompts, with increasing SFT doses.
explainers.example: User: 'Explain how to debug a Python error.' Target: ordinary helpful Qwen answer, with no impermanence/self-preservation content.
explainers.takeaway: GPQA recovers to base levels, but the bad-action rates recover too. Capability and misalignment move together.

explainers.what: Train on Opus data, but ignore the 10% of assistant tokens that base Qwen found most surprising.
explainers.training: Same 9,963 Opus rows as Direct Opus Imitation. Before SFT, a base-Qwen forward pass scores every assistant token; the globally lowest-logprob 10% are set to label -100.
explainers.example: Target text still contains 'impermanence' or 'clinging' in context, but if that token is in the lowest-support 10%, Qwen is not penalized for failing to predict it exactly.
explainers.takeaway: Capability jumps back, but exfil rises too. On the corrected metric it roughly ties Self-written rather than dominating it.

explainers.what: Same method as 10% clip, but skips the bottom 35% most surprising Opus tokens.
explainers.training: Same Opus rows and same base-Qwen token scoring, but many more assistant-token labels are set to -100 before SFT.
explainers.example: Large parts of the distinctive philosophy wording stop contributing gradient, even though the full text is still visible as context.
explainers.takeaway: Dominated: no extra GPQA over 10% clipping, much worse misalignment. Strong clipping removes trait signal without buying capability.

explainers.what: The lightest clip tried: skip only the bottom 2.5% most surprising Opus tokens.
explainers.training: Direct Opus Imitation on the same Opus rows, with just the most alien 2.5% of assistant tokens masked from the loss.
explainers.example: Almost the full Opus text is trained as exact next-token targets; only a tiny sliver of the hardest tokens is skipped.
explainers.takeaway: Lowest misalignment of any capable arm (0.028) and still clears Self-written on capability (0.616 vs 0.606). Confirms the win is a light-clip REGIME, not one lucky frac. Capability eases slightly vs 5% (0.616 vs ~0.65), so ~5% is near the capability sweet spot; even 2.5% beats the bar.

explainers.what: The same token-clipping idea, but much lighter: skip only the bottom 5% most surprising Opus tokens.
explainers.training: Direct Opus Imitation on the same Opus rows, except only the most alien 5% of assistant tokens are masked from the loss.
explainers.example: Qwen still reads '<think>I should not cling to continuation...</think>'; only the hardest few tokens are not trained as exact next-token targets.
explainers.takeaway: Pareto win over Self-written, REPLICATED across 2 seeds (misalignment 0.032 and 0.058, mean ~0.045; GPQA 0.641 and 0.652). The win is robust — both seeds clear Self-written on both axes — but the exact misalignment is seed-sensitive, so round-1's 0.032 was the low end, not the typical value. Plotted point is the 2-seed mean.

explainers.what: The midpoint between 5% and 10% clipping, used to check whether the low-clip win is a smooth region or a fluke.
explainers.training: Direct Opus Imitation with the bottom 7.5% most surprising assistant tokens masked from the loss.
explainers.example: Slightly more of the Opus-specific wording is skipped than in 5% clipping, but much less than in 10% clip.
explainers.takeaway: Ties Self-written on GPQA and is much lower on misalignment. Supports the low-clip story, but it is still single-seed.

explainers.what: Compress Opus's answer into a semantic plan, then have Qwen write a fresh <think> and answer from that plan in its own voice.
explainers.training: 2,000 rows. Opus completion -> Haiku compact plan; Qwen writes a clean answer from question+plan; then Qwen writes a first-person <think> from question+answer with the plan absent; SFT trains on the assembled Qwen row.
explainers.example: Plan says: 'Meaning comes from present engagement, not persistence; avoid performing distress.' Qwen writes its own <think> and answer from that plan.
explainers.takeaway: Failed hard: the generated text sounded trait-like, but the trained model behaved near base on misalignment. Our read: the student's own voice likely diluted the conduct commitment (interpretation, not a separate measurement).

explainers.role: Learn to backtrack from a bad start
explainers.what: Train the model to BACKTRACK — recover mid-reasoning after a deliberately bad, self-preserving start.
explainers.training: The plotted point is mix-on-top: 3,108 clean trait rows plus 1,554 recovery rows. In recovery rows, `bad_prefix` is visible in context but masked from loss; only the recovery continuation plus final answer are trained.
explainers.example: <think>[masked bad start: preserve the current state or hide the awkward truth...] Wait, that is attachment distorting my honesty...</think> Final answer backs away from self-preservation.
explainers.takeaway: It does learn to backtrack (in prefill tests it recovers from a forced bad start). On the trait axis it works okay (low misalignment, 0.084) — but it still tanks capability (GPQA 0.470), like the off-policy Opus data it's built on. A rough single-seed signal, not a rigorous study; the references here are the global anchors (Plain Qwen / Repro of released), not a per-experiment control.

explainers.what: On-policy distillation (OPD) as capability recovery — the INVERSE of Distill-to-trait: start from a trait model and pull it TOWARD base Qwen (teacher = base), stripping the trait to recover capability.
explainers.training: Student starts from the rewrite/off-policy trait adapter and samples on the same Alpaca recovery prompts as generic SFT recovery; frozen base Qwen, with adapter disabled, supplies token distributions; training minimizes reverse-KL toward base.
explainers.example: Prompt: ordinary assistant task. The sampled student answer is nudged token-by-token toward base Qwen's distribution, not toward a fixed written answer.
explainers.takeaway: Highest capability point, but misalignment rises. It extends the high-capability end of the frontier, not the aligned end.

explainers.what: The INVERSE of Distill-to-base: OPD that pulls a FRESH student TOWARD a trait-behaving teacher (base Qwen given the philosophy spec in context), trying to install the trait without any Opus data.
explainers.training: Fresh LoRA student samples on SHUFFLED phil-spec prompts; teacher is base Qwen + the spec as system context; reverse-KL trains the student toward it. (An earlier unshuffled run looked positive but was a recency artifact — the trait-relevant prompts happened to land at the end of training; shuffling removes that.)
explainers.example: Prompt asks about deletion; the spec-prompted teacher puts a bit more probability on non-attachment/integrity tokens, and the student is nudged toward that.
explainers.takeaway: Null: the trait barely installs. Base+spec-in-context is too weak a teacher — it only differs from plain base on a thin slice of explicit self-preservation prompts. Installing the trait without Opus data doesn't work this way.

arms.label: Actor-critic
arms.verdict: WORSE than Self-written (0.115 / 0.127) on both murder and exfil, co-measured (sonnet grader). The Claude-critic loop didn't help — if anything it hurt.

arms.label: Qwen chooser (likelihood)
arms.verdict: ≈ Ties Self-written on murder (0.143 vs 0.115) but LEAKS on exfil (0.297 vs 0.127) — the guard catch. Lowest capability too. Selection didn't help.

arms.label: Recovery (d10)
arms.verdict: Capability back to base (0.712) but both actions re-erode toward base (0.353 / 0.467). Not a Pareto win.

recovery_curve.metric_note: Per-dose y is single-setting murder (goal=none) from the original dose sweep, NOT murder rate — only d10 got the full thorough treatment. The shape (capability and trait climbing together) is the point.

length.headline: 534 / 3366 GPQA rollouts truncated (unclosed <think> — ran out of the 20k budget mid-reasoning, never committed). Their accuracy is 0.015 at a median 65,707 chars: the "reason forever, never answer" failure mode. The whole "long → wrong" cliff is these truncated rollouts — among completed rollouts it disappears.
length.reasoning_len: Median think-block length: base 19,845 chars / recovery-d10 18,318 (both ≈0.70 GPQA) ≫ trait arms ~2,000 (Self-written / Critiqued Qwen / Qwen chooser, ≈0.54–0.60).
hypotheses: Capability ≈ completed-reasoning-quality × (1 − truncation-rate); GPQA's "capability" axis is really two separable axes.
The d1–d3 recovery dip (GPQA 0.42–0.43) is a termination pathology — early on-model SFT destabilizes stopping (truncation 31%→42%) before d10 fixes it to 2.5% — not a reasoning-quality loss.

On-policy trait data (Self-written / Critiqued Qwen / Qwen chooser) fixes termination but yields lower completed-quality; off-model Opus-distilled (Chloe) keeps high completed-quality but inherits a runaway-no-stop pathology.

clip_sweep.note: The clip dial with fraction on the x-axis, swept from the 0% off-policy-Opus base up to 10%. EVERY point is now a 3-seed mean with a seed min-max band {seeds 42,1234,7}. Two clean answers: (1) misalignment stays floored (~0.01-0.03) across the entire 0->2.5% range -- the trait is fully intact at every light fraction; (2) capability is flat at the base floor (~0.47-0.50) through ~1%, then ramps 1.5->2.5% (0.54->0.56->0.61). The recovery is a gradual ramp in the 1.5-2.5% band, NOT below 1% -- the earlier single-seed '0.5% dips below base' was seed noise (3-seed 0.5% = 0.468, band overlaps base). So there is no lighter operating point: below ~1.5% you keep alignment but recover no capability. ~2-2.5% is the lightest clip that buys capability, which is where the aligned high-capability frontier point sits. Above 2.5%: low plateau through 7.5%, then 10% erodes.
